title: Beer on Upholstery
stainChemistry: Beer is a low-pigment tannin stain — most fresh spills on upholstery clear with cool damp cloth + NSD alone. The upholstery-specific risk isn't the stain chemistry; it's the LIQUID VOLUME. Beer comes in larger spills than wine or coffee, so more liquid = more risk of batting saturation + water ring. Check the upholstery code tag (W / W-S / S / X) first. Aged beer develops a faint tan ring as sugar caramelizes and residual polyphenol oxidizes.
whyThisWorks: Damp-cloth transfer, never pour. Cold NSD handles surface sugar + barley-protein residue in most fresh spills. Aged stains need the acid-side tannin spotter + optional peroxide on whites. Weighted dry towel between passes protects the batting from ringing.
defaultAssumption: Fresh beer on W / W-S upholstery is a fast, easy job. S-code or X-code = call a pro. The real risk is flooding the batting with too much liquid during treatment — damp cloth only.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Care tag, clean white cotton towel
spottingProtocol.instruction: Read upholstery code. W / W-S: proceed. S-only: solvent lane (not for beer). X: vacuum only. Press a folded white towel straight down on the spill — extract as much as possible before chemistry. Outside-in.

spottingProtocol.equipment: NSD in cold water, damp cloth, weighted dry towel
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply cold NSD via DAMP cloth. Press, lift, rotate. Never pour. Weighted dry towel 5 min on top to extract. 2–3 cycles for fresh beer — usually enough.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Professional tannin spotter (Chemspec Tannin Spotter or PRENETT-A) — acid-side tannin treatment
spottingProtocol.instruction: Only for aged stains where tan ring remains. Acid-side spotter by dropper. Never pour. Blot with dry towel. Fresh beer skips this step.
For aged tan ring on light / white upholstery: 3% H₂O₂ drop-by-drop. Patch-test hidden area first. Skip on dark / patterned.

spottingProtocol.equipment: NSD damp cloth, heavy dry towel + weight
spottingProtocol.instruction: NSD-damp cloth. Weighted dry towel 20 min to pull moisture up from batting.
Fan-dry. Don't sit on it until bone-dry. Inspect next day for wicking.

steps: 1. Read code tag. W / W-S: proceed. S / X: pro.
3. Cold NSD via damp cloth + weighted extract.
4. Aged ring: acid-side tannin spotter (Chemspec Tannin Spotter / PRENETT-A).
5. Aged on light: 3% H₂O₂ on the ring.
products: Professional tannin spotter (Chemspec Tannin Spotter or PRENETT-A) — aged only
warnings: Beer is low-pigment — don't over-treat fresh spills
Damp cloth only — never pour, batting saturates fast with beer's volume
Patch test peroxide on colored upholstery

homeSolutions.instruction: Find the care tag under a cushion. W or W-S: proceed. S-only: solvent upholstery, not a beer DIY — call a pro. X: vacuum only, also a pro.

Press a thick stack of white paper towels or folded white cloth straight down on the beer spill. Lift, dry section, press again. Outside-in. Beer is a lot of volume — get as much out BEFORE you do any chemistry.

homeSolutions.agent: Cold Water + Dawn on a DAMP Cloth — Never Pour
homeSolutions.instruction: Dampen a white cloth with cool water + a drop of Dawn. Press on the spot, lift, rotate. Never pour water on upholstery — beer volumes plus pour = batting saturation + permanent water ring. Keep cloth damp, not dripping.

homeSolutions.agent: Weighted Dry Towel Between Wet Passes
homeSolutions.instruction: After each wet dab pass, press a thick DRY towel with a book or pan on top for 15–20 minutes. Pulls moisture up out of the batting. This matters MORE on upholstery than most surfaces because a beer-sized spill can saturate batting if you're not careful.

homeSolutions.agent: Aged Tan Ring? One Extra Step
homeSolutions.instruction: If beer sat overnight and left a tan ring: dab a dilute white-vinegar solution (1 tsp vinegar + 3 tbsp cool water) on the ring via damp cloth, 3 minutes, blot. For stubborn aged ring on light / white upholstery only, 3% drugstore hydrogen peroxide dab 3–5 min, blot. Skip peroxide on dark or patterned upholstery.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never mix peroxide with chlorine bleach or ammonia. Patch test on colored upholstery.

homeSolutions.instruction: Point a fan at the spot. Don't sit on the furniture until fully dry — several hours. Inspect next day for a tan ring wicking back. If the ring returns, batting is saturated and a pro extraction is the call.

homeSolutions.agent: Escalate for S-Code, Wool, or Wicking
homeSolutions.instruction: Escalate for S-code / X-code / wool upholstery, if the ring wicked back, or if two home rounds didn't clear it. Tell them: "Beer on [W / W-S / code unknown] upholstery, age [fresh / overnight / older], home-treated with Dawn + [vinegar / 3% peroxide for aged ring] + weighted towel."

steps: 1. Check upholstery code tag. W / W-S: proceed. S / X: pro.
2. Blot extract straight down, no rub.
3. Cold water + Dawn on a DAMP cloth — never pour.
4. Weighted dry towel 15–20 min between wet passes.
5. Aged ring: diluted vinegar, then optional 3% peroxide on light upholstery.
7. Escalate for S-code, wool, wicking, or plateau.
products: 3% hydrogen peroxide (aged ring, light upholstery only)
Heavy book or pan for weighted drying
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop for S / X code, wool upholstery, wicking, patch-test failure, or two-round plateau.
warnings: NEVER pour — beer volume saturates batting fast
NEVER mix peroxide with chlorine bleach or ammonia

neverDo: Never rub — spreads beer into clean areas
fiberSensitivities: W / W-S upholstery: tolerates standard chemistry
Silk / velvet: escalate even on W-code

materialWarnings: Damp cloth only — beer volume saturates batting

escalation.when: S / X code upholstery, wool, stain wicks back after drying, patch test fails, or two home rounds plateau.
escalation.whatToTell: "Beer on [W / W-S / code unknown] upholstery, age [fresh / overnight / older], home-treated with Dawn + [vinegar / 3% H₂O₂ for aged ring] + weighted towel."

contextQuestions: Upholstery code — W, W-S, S, or X?
Fresh spill or overnight with tan ring?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Beer is a low-pigment stain — easy chemistry. The risk on upholstery is VOLUME — beer spills are larger than wine or coffee, and batting saturation is the real failure mode. Damp cloth + weighted extract is the whole discipline.
